code: export function Relation({ from, to, label, }: { from: AnyNodeRef; to: AnyNodeRef; label?: string | undefined; }): any {}
export function Doc({ explains, priority, children, }: { explains: string; priority?: DocPriority | undefined; children: any; }): any {}
export function H1({ children }: { children: any; }): any {}
export function H2({ children }: { children: any; }): any {}
export function H3({ children }: { children: any; }): any {}
export function P({ children }: { children: any; }): any {}
export function List({ children }: { children: any; }): any {}

export function Adr({ id, title, status, date, deciders, children, }: { id: string; title: string; status: "proposed" | "accepted" | "deprecated" | "superseded"; date?: string | undefined; deciders?: string | undefined; children: any; }): any {}

export function Step({ node, label }: { node: AnyNodeRef; label?: string | undefined; }): any {}

"description": "TS Knowledge Base. A TypeScript-native DSL for expressing architectural intent as typed declarations. Produces renderable docs/diagrams and a queryable knowledge graph, supporting type-checked snippet references, semantic relations, and constraints.",

exports.description: A placeholder for referencing anything from the global tskb registry into jsx documentation tags

files.label: md
files.description: The main npm README.md for the tskb package

code: Let your **AI assistant document your codebase** as it works. Architecture knowledge accumulates in a **compiler-verified artifact** — query it, ask questions about your system, navigate it with your AI.
> **Your AI writes the docs. You navigate them.**
npx tskb init           # interactive — pass --yes to accept all defaults
npm run docs            # added to package.json by `init`
An empty graph, an interactive D3 explorer in the browser, and AI skills wired into Claude Code and GitHub Copilot. The codebase map fills in as you (and your AI) write `.tskb.tsx` docs over time.
- **Stable:** CLI commands (`init`, `build`, `ls`, `pick`, `search`, `docs`, `flows`, `context`, `explore`), JSX components, registry primitives, graph schema, generated skill/instruction files.
- **May still evolve:** internal extraction APIs, explorer chunk format, additional node types.
Pin a minor version if you script against the JSON output.
A TypeScript-native DSL for expressing **architectural intent** as code.

Your AI writes and maintains documentation in `.tskb.tsx` files that are **type-checked by the TypeScript compiler** and reference **real folders, files, and exports** via `typeof import()`. The result is a **structured knowledge graph** you can query with the CLI, browse in the explorer, or feed back to your AI — all from the same source of truth.
> Refactor your code → stale docs break at compile time. The AI fixes them.
Large TypeScript codebases drift in predictable ways:
- **Tribal knowledge** — architecture lives in senior engineers' heads
- **Documentation decay** — Markdown and diagrams silently fall out of sync
- **Compound complexity** — onboarding cost grows faster than the codebase
tskb makes architecture documentation **typed, validated, and enforced at build time** — so it can't quietly rot.
Declare structural elements once in the global `tskb` namespace. Six primitives cover the surface:
| `Folder`   | A logical area (feature, layer, package)          | `path` must exist on disk   |
| `Module`   | A source file, with type-safe access to its shape | `typeof import("…")`        |
| `Export`   | A named export (class, fn, const)                 | `typeof import("…").Symbol` |
| `File`     | A non-TS/JS file (README, config, spec)           | `path` must exist on disk   |
| `Term`     | A domain concept not tied to a specific file      | string literal type         |
| `External` | A third-party service or package                  | free-form key-value props   |
import type { Folder, Module, Export, Term } from "tskb";
jwt: Term<"JSON Web Tokens for stateless authentication">;
`typeof import()` means the compiler validates every reference. Rename `AuthService` → the doc build breaks.
`.tskb.tsx` files reference the vocabulary and add prose, snippets, flows, relations, and decisions.
import { Doc, H1, P, Snippet, ref } from "tskb";
// `ref` is a typed sentinel — the cast tells TS which registered node this identifier represents.
<Doc explains="Login flow and JWT issuance" priority="essential">
`<Snippet>` is **not** a string literal — its body is fully type-checked. If `login()` changes signature, the doc build fails.
Named, ordered sequences through the system — login pipelines, build steps, request paths. They become first-class graph nodes.
// AuthMiddleware, AuthService, and UserRepository are declared in your vocabulary
// the same way AuthService is declared above, then pulled in via `ref as tskb.Exports[...]`.
<Flow name="login" desc="HTTP request to session token" priority="essential">
Express explicit semantic edges between any two nodes, and capture the _why_ behind decisions:
<Relation from={AuthService} to={Postgres} label="reads user records from" />
title="Use JWT instead of session cookies"
Stateless tokens let us scale the auth tier horizontally without sticky sessions.
Three levels control what the AI sees first:
- `essential` — surfaced in generated AI skills and `ls` output
- `constraint` — architectural rules; surfaced in `pick`/`search` so they can't be missed before changes

**Authored intent** — every JSX reference creates a typed edge. `

{AuthService} uses {UserRepository}

` records edges for both. `` adds an explicit `related-to` edge.
**Inferred structure** — folder hierarchy, module imports, export ownership, and file containment are read from the filesystem and TypeScript AST.
The result combines what you _meant_ with what's _actually there_.
npx tskb init                            # Interactive scaffolder (--yes for defaults)
npm run docs                             # Build the graph (script added by init)
npx tskb build "<glob>" --project <name> --tsconfig <path>   # Direct build invocation
npx tskb ls --depth 4                    # Folder tree + essential docs
npx tskb pick "AuthService"              # Full detail for one node
npx tskb search "auth"                   # Fuzzy search across the graph
npx tskb context "ServiceLayer"          # Node + neighborhood + docs (one call)
npx tskb flows                           # List flows by priority
npx tskb explore                         # Open the interactive explorer
npx tskb explore --export ./public       # Bake static site
All commands accept `--plain` (~30% fewer tokens for AI consumption) and `--optimized` (compact JSON).
`context` is the most efficient single call — it returns the target node, its neighborhood, and all referencing docs in one shot.

`tskb explore` serves a D3-powered SPA that renders the graph as an interactive diagram — folders nest, modules expand, edges trace references and imports. Ships pre-built; no extra install. `--export` writes a self-contained static folder you can drop on any host.
The build emits skills/instructions for Claude Code and GitHub Copilot, regenerated from the graph on every build — never edit the files manually.
| **tskb**               | Codebase map and CLI orientation. Always-on entry point.                                       |
| **tskb-toc**           | Repo table of contents: boundaries, externals, flows, essential-docs index. Loaded on demand.  |
| **tskb-update**        | When and where to write `.tskb.tsx` docs — workflow, folder structure, authoring rules.        |
| **tskb-update-syntax** | Full authoring syntax reference: registry primitives, JSX components, snippets, class methods. |
Enable them during `npx tskb init` or create `.claude/skills/` and `.github/` before the next build.

At the start of a session the assistant already has the folder tree and essential docs. For anything deeper it runs `search`, `context`, or `pick` itself. Each session adds to the graph; the next session sees what's already there.
> **You:** "I'm adding JWT auth in `src/services/auth.service.ts`. Document it."
> 1. Runs `tskb search "auth"` and `tskb context "src/services"` — checks what's already mapped.
> 2. Proposes the questions worth answering: _"How does login issue a JWT?"_, _"What guarantees token expiry?"_
> 3. You pick which questions are real. The AI declares new vocabulary and writes one `.tskb.tsx` per question.
> 4. Runs `npm run docs`. The compiler validates every reference and snippet.
> **Next session:** the AI finds the auth doc via `tskb search`, references `AuthService` directly — no re-reading the code.
folders, modules, exports, files, terms, flows, docs   // typed records
The graph is the primary output. The explorer and AI skill files are all derived from it.
**vs Markdown / ADRs** — type-checked against real code, not free text that drifts.
**vs Structurizr / C4 / PlantUML** — native TypeScript instead of a custom DSL; produces a queryable graph, not just static diagrams.
**vs TypeDoc** — documents _architecture and intent_ (why things exist, how they relate), not API surfaces.
Unique to tskb: compiler-validated references via `typeof import()`, type-checked code snippets, first-class flows, doc priority, an interactive explorer, and AI skills generated from the graph.

- **First-class JSX for common architecture patterns** — purpose-built components for `<Layered>`, `<Hexagonal>`, `<EventDriven>`, `<Cqrs>`, `<StateMachine>`, `<DDD>`, and pipeline / saga / pub-sub variants. Each emits typed graph nodes and edges, surfaces violations at build time, and renders distinctly in the explorer.
